frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: sensitivity (d_a) in Y/N detection indexes subjective perception

frameworks.name: blindsight dissociation
frameworks.one_line: subjective perception dissociable from general perceptual capacity

frameworks.name: working-memory-perception interaction
frameworks.one_line: WM content modulates perceptual processing via congruency

core_question: Does the content of working memory selectively modulate subjective perception when general perceptual processing capacity is held constant?
key_assumptions: 2-IFC titration equates general perceptual processing capacity across conditions
Subjective perception and perceptual capacity are dissociable (blindsight motivation)
manipulations: Congruency between working-memory content and perceptual content (categorically congruent vs incongruent)
Stimulus contrast titrated to match 2-IFC performance
key_findings: No evidence that WM congruency selectively modulated Y/N sensitivity (d_a) when 2-IFC capacity was matched
Result contradicts a prior n=8 pilot trend of selective impairment under incongruent WM
interpretations: Discrepancy with the pilot motivates a pre-registered replication on the original pilot design
limitations_noted: Discrepancy between current (n=16) result and earlier pilot (n=8) is unresolved
